---
title: 'Job&#39;s History and Family'
date: '2013-10-07T05:37:00.0000000+00:00'
draft: true
blogger_blog: 'Darrell Wolfe, Storyteller'
blogger_status: 'LIVE'
blogger_post_id: '8539201759506276174'
blogger_filename: '/2013/10/job-history-and-family.html'
blogger_source: 'takeout-20260223T235336Z-3-001.zip:Takeout/Blogger/Blogs/Darrell Wolfe, Storyteller/feed.atom'
categories:
  - 'Job Research'
  - 'Job'
---

For the record, I have not written any of this information. I am pasting it here in one place to get back to it easier. I am doing research on Job. The Following Excerpts are the most exhaustive study I've seen of Job so far in my research and I believe this author(s) are on the right track in some of their assumptions.<br /><br />Please see the links provided to find their full study.<br /><br />Job's father is Issachar, whose father is Jacob/Israel (Job's grandfather), whose  father is Issac (Job's Great Grandfather), whose  father is Abraham (Jobs Great Great Grandfather). <br /><h1 class="Title TitleSmall"><span style="font-size: small;"><a href="http://www.biblestudytools.com/kjv/genesis/passage.aspx?q=genesis+46:8-18" target="_blank">Genesis 46:8-18 (King James Version)</a></span></h1><span class="versetext" id="ge46-8" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">8</span>    And these are the <span class="strongs">names</span> of the <span class="strongs">children</span> of <span class="strongs">Israel,</span> which <span class="strongs">came</span>  into <span class="strongs">Egypt,</span> <span class="strongs">Jacob</span> and his <span class="strongs">sons:</span> <span class="strongs">Reuben,</span> <span class="strongs">Jacob's</span> <span class="strongs">firstborn.</span>   </span>          <span class="versetext" id="ge46-9" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">9</span>    And the <span class="strongs">sons</span> of <span class="strongs">Reuben;</span> <span class="strongs">Hanoch,</span> and <span class="strongs">Phallu,</span> and <span class="strongs">Hezron,</span> and <span class="strongs">Carmi.</span>   </span>          <span class="versetext" id="ge46-10" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">10</span>    And the <span class="strongs">sons</span> of <span class="strongs">Simeon;</span> <span class="strongs">Jemuel,</span><a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="a"></a> and <span class="strongs">Jamin,</span> and <span class="strongs">Ohad,</span> and <span class="strongs">Jachin,</span> and <span class="strongs">Zohar,</span> and <span class="strongs">Shaul</span> the <span class="strongs">son</span> of a <span class="strongs">Canaanitish</span> woman.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="ge46-11" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">11</span>    And the <span class="strongs">sons</span> of <span class="strongs">Levi;</span> <span class="strongs">Gershon,</span><a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="b"></a> <span class="strongs">Kohath,</span> and <span class="strongs">Merari.</span>   </span>          <span class="versetext" id="ge46-12" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">12</span>    And the <span class="strongs">sons</span> of <span class="strongs">Judah;</span> <span class="strongs">Er,</span> and <span class="strongs">Onan,</span> and <span class="strongs">Shelah,</span> and <span class="strongs">Pharez,</span> and <span class="strongs">Zerah:</span> but <span class="strongs">Er</span> and <span class="strongs">Onan</span> <span class="strongs">died</span>  in the <span class="strongs">land</span> of <span class="strongs">Canaan.</span> And the <span class="strongs">sons</span> of <span class="strongs">Pharez</span> were <span class="strongs">Hezron</span> and <span class="strongs">Hamul.</span>   </span>          <span class="versetext highlightThenFade" id="ge46-13"><span class="versenum">13</span>    A<span style="background-color: yellow;"><b>nd the <span class="strongs">sons</span> of <span class="strongs">Issachar;</span> <span class="strongs">Tola,</span> and <span class="strongs">Phuvah,</span> and <span class="strongs"><span style="font-size: large;">Job</span>,</span><a class="highlightThenFade" href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="c"></a> and </b></span><span class="strongs"><span style="background-color: yellow;"><b>Shimron</b></span>.</span>   </span>          <span class="versetext" id="ge46-14" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">14</span>    And the <span class="strongs">sons</span> of <span class="strongs">Zebulun;</span> <span class="strongs">Sered,</span> and <span class="strongs">Elon,</span> and <span class="strongs">Jahleel.</span>   </span>          <span class="versetext" id="ge46-15" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">15</span>    These be the <span class="strongs">sons</span> of <span class="strongs">Leah,</span> which she <span class="strongs">bare</span>  unto <span class="strongs">Jacob</span> in <span class="strongs">Padanaram,</span> with his <span class="strongs">daughter</span> <span class="strongs">Dinah:</span> all the <span class="strongs">souls</span> of his <span class="strongs">sons</span> and his <span class="strongs">daughters</span> were <span class="strongs">thirty</span> and <span class="strongs">three.</span>   </span>          <span class="versetext" id="ge46-16" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">16</span>    And the <span class="strongs">sons</span> of <span class="strongs">Gad;</span> <span class="strongs">Ziphion,</span><a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="d"></a> and <span class="strongs">Haggi,</span> <span class="strongs">Shuni,</span> and <span class="strongs">Ezbon,</span> <span class="strongs">Eri,</span> and <span class="strongs">Arodi,</span> and <span class="strongs">Areli.</span>   </span>          <span class="versetext" id="ge46-17" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">17</span>    And the <span class="strongs">sons</span> of <span class="strongs">Asher;</span> <span class="strongs">Jimnah,</span> and <span class="strongs">Ishuah,</span> and <span class="strongs">Isui,</span> and <span class="strongs">Beriah,</span> and <span class="strongs">Serah</span> their <span class="strongs">sister:</span> and the <span class="strongs">sons</span> of <span class="strongs">Beriah;</span> <span class="strongs">Heber,</span> and <span class="strongs">Malchiel.</span>   </span>          <span class="versetext" id="ge46-18" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">18</span>    These are the <span class="strongs">sons</span> of <span class="strongs">Zilpah,</span> whom <span class="strongs">Laban</span> <span class="strongs">gave</span>  to <span class="strongs">Leah</span> his <span class="strongs">daughter,</span> and these she <span class="strongs">bare</span>  unto <span class="strongs">Jacob,</span> even <span class="strongs">sixteen</span> <span class="strongs"></span>  <span class="strongs">souls.</span>&nbsp;    </span><br /><br /><a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/2010/09/righteous-job-and-his-kin.html">http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/2010/09/righteous-job-and-his-kin.html</a><br /><h2 class="date-header">Sunday, September 26, 2010</h2><a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="1673199973007474148"></a> <br /><h3 class="post-title entry-title" itemprop="name">Righteous Job and His Kin </h3><div class="post-header"></div><div class="post-body entry-content" id="post-body-1673199973007474148" itemprop="description articleBody"><div dir="ltr" style="text-align: left;"><br />Alice C. Linsley<br /><br /><br />Job is a difficult book to classify. Job is regarded as a prophet  but his book is not like the books of the Prophets.  Job is often  classified as a wisdom literature, but unlike other books of wisdom it  is about an historical debate and involves historical persons.  The  debate probably dates to a later time than Job and his friends actually  lived. The author ridicules Job's friends as people from the desolate  wilderness who live in the clefts of the valleys and in the caves.  They  are portrayed as donkeys braying among the bushes (Job 30:3-7). This is  not how Job himself would have regarded his kin.  We can be fairly  certain that the author of Job was not someone who lived in Arabia or  even in that part of Canaan that was <a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/2009/05/horite-territory.html">Horite territory</a>.<br /><br />The  first two chapters of Job provide a clue as to when the author lived.   Here we find a picture of Satan as a "son of God" who has the power to  accuse. This suggests an author from the <a href="http://www.infidels.org/library/modern/gerald_larue/otll/chap24.html">Persian Period</a>,  writing long after the time that Job, Eliphaz, Bildad, Zophar and Elihu  would have lived. Satan as the accuser was a favorite theme of that  period.<br /><br />Job reasons that God is not as his friends describe Him.  In fact, a close reading of Job will dispel most notions of God, whether  ancient or modern. God is not cruel like the pagan gods. God is not  impersonal as conceived by the Deists.  God is not deceived, as sinners  hope.  God is not blind to the suffering and death of the righteous. He  recognized the blood of Abel which cried to Him from the ground and He  knows of every tear and drop of blood shed by His righteous ones. Nor is  God the Divine Arbitrator of karma as in Hinduism and Buddhism.<br /><br />Yet  Job never claims to know God's mind. For the righteous, suffering gives  way to contemplating the "secret councils of God" (Job 15:8) and to  prayer that we might not experience the second death of which Baruch  2:17 speaks: "The dead who are in the graves, whose souls [<a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/2010/01/phrase-bosom-of-abraham-refers-to-place.html">ka</a>]  are taken from their bodies, will give unto the Lord neither praise nor  righteousness." Abraham's people believed that the soul and the body  must stay joined after death to enjoy eternal life. This is Job's last  hope. He proclaims that "I have a living Defender and that he will rise  up last [as Job's final witness] on the dust of the earth. After my  awakening, he will set me close to him, and from my flesh I shall look  on God. He whom I shall see with take my part; my eyes will be gazing on  no stranger." (Job 19:25-27 NJB) In Job 13:16, Job declares that his  trust in God as his salvation (Yeshua = Jesus) will never be destroyed.  Job comes to this in his suffering, but his friends are not suffering.  For them this life is good and Job's suffering can only be explained has  having lost God's favor. They said the same about Jesus in His passion.<br /><br />Eliphaz  was a descendant of Teman, one of Esau's sons (Gen. 36).  The Temanites  were known for their wisdom. Jeremiah 49 links the Temanites with the  Dedanites. According to Genesis 10:7 and Genesis 25:3, Dedan was  descended from Kush and from Abraham by his <a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/2008/06/keturah-wife-or-concubine.html">cousin wife, Keturah</a>.  Dedan's father was Abraham's first-born son Joktan.  Dedan's brother  was Sheba the Younger. Isaiah 21:13 alludes to the "caravans of  Dedanites" in Arabia, and Ezekiel 27:20 speaks of Dedan as supplying  Tyre with precious things. Dedan is associated with Uz in the hill  country of Edom, Job's homeland.  This is <a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/2010/09/abrahams-nephews-and-niece.html">Uz the Elder</a>,  son of Nahor, whose grandson (by his daughter) was Uz the son of Dishan  (I Chron. 1:42). Dishan was a son of Seir the Horite. Uz the Younger  was Seir's grandson. Here is Seir's Horite family:<br /><br /><br /><br /><a href="/images/imported/blogger-googleusercontent-com/gen36-of-esau-24752fbd170c.gif"><img border="0" src="/images/imported/blogger-googleusercontent-com/gen36-of-esau-88e82c26d229.gif" /></a><br /><br /><br /><br /><br />So  we know that Job had Horite blood. The Horites were devotees of Horus  who was called "the son of God" and "Horus of 2 Crowns".  Uz, Buz and  Huz represent a 3-clan Horite confederation based on kinship.<br /><br />The  trial of Job in which Satan acts as the accuser parallels Zechariah  3:2-6 where Satan accuses the High Priest Joshua (Yeshua).  In that  trial God acquits Joshua and commands that he be clothed in clean  garments and crowned with 2 crowns (ataroth). This points to Jesus who  as the Son of God would wear <a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/2010/06/exacators-at-hierakonpolis-report.html">2 crowns according to Horite belief</a>.<br /><br />The  Horite confederation is not identified as Uz, Huz and Buz, but rather  as Dedan, Tema and Buz. The oldest Arabic script emanated from the North  Arabian oases of Tema and Dedan in the Hijaz. Tema is known by Arabs as  Taima and lies about 70 miles north-east of Dedan. Tema, Dedan and  Dumah were caravan stops along the trade route from Sheba to Babylon.  The Dedanites were famous for mining.<br /><br />Job's kin shared appearance  and life style. They dwelt in hills and built shrines in caves (such as  at Petra) and the men shaved their heads (Jeremiah 25:23), as did  Horite priests. This suggests that this was a confederation of Horite  priestly families. Genesis 36 confirms this, listing Uz's grandson Dedan  as a Horite ruler. Here we also find reference to Huz or Husham of the  land of Tema (Gen. 36:34).<br /><br />Bildah the "Shuhite" was a descendant  of Abraham's son Shuah (Gen. 25:2). Zophar the "Naamathite" was a  descendent of Naamah, the daughter of Lamech (Gen. 4:22) who married her  patrilineal cousin Methuselah (Gen. 5:25).<br /><br />The last of Job's kin  to speak is the young man Elihu. The name Elihu, which appears only in  Job, is a priestly name. In Strong's Concordance Elihu is said to mean  "He is my God".  However, it is more likely that the name relates to  God's Word since El refers to God and <a href="http://biblicalanthropology.blogspot.com/2010/09/te-huts-victory-over-te-hom.html">Hu </a>was the ancient Egyptian/Horite word for the divine Word that overcomes chaos. This fits the context of the book of Job.<br /><br />Elihu  is of the clan of Buz. I Chronicles 5:14 tells us that the son of Buz  was Jahdo and Jahdo's son was Yeshishai, the Aramaic form of  Yeshua/Jesus. Elihu is the mysterious figure whose speeches fill the  last chapters of the book of Job. He was likely the brother-in-law of  Judah's grandson Hezron. This suggests that Elihu lived with his father  Barachel in Buz but was Ram's heir. Ram was his maternal grandfather and  the high priest. (Ram means "high.") This means that Elihu was a  ruler-priest and an ancestor of David. It is Elihu who takes us beyond  the wisdom of Eliphaz, Bildad and Zophar. He moves us from the  retributive justice of Job's 3 friends to the reality that "God is  greater than any human beieng. Why then quarrel with Him for not  replying to you word for word? God speaks first one way and then in  another, although we do not realise it." (Job 33:12-14NJB) Elihu's 230  line discourse elaborates and illustrates how this is true.<br /><br />Job  and all his friends are descendants of Enoch, a man who is commended for  his faith in Hebrews 11: 6. Each had his own answer to why Job ws  suffering, but only the suffering Job glimpsed something of the mystery  of God's counsel. As a man of faith, Job remembers that "God watched  over me" (Job 29:2) and that "the friendly counsel of God was over my  tent" (29:4), and this sustains him in his suffering.  Those who accuse  him are his own kin, as was true with our Lord Jesus. Their accusations  are the same as those used by the Jewish rulers to accuse Jesus, the  Righteous One.<br /><br />The central message of Job is to remember God at  all times, even as we hope to be remembered in the eternal Kingdom.  It  is to love God whether enjoying life or afflicted, and to look to Jesus  Christ, the Author and Finisher of our faith. In this sense, Job is a  prophetic book which presents wisdom of the deepest kind.  Patrick Henry  Reardon recognizes that Job moves toward the "Bible's apocalytic  principle", that is to say, "More is happening than seems to be  happening." (The Trial of Job, p. 46).<br /><div></div><div></div><div>Related reading: &nbsp;<a href="http://www.communitychurchva.info/Forms/BookofJOB.pdf">Job's Friends and Their Contribution to the Message of Job</a>; <a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/2010/10/abraham-and-job-horite-rulers.html">Abraham and Job: Horite Rulers</a></div><div></div></div></div><div class="post-footer-line post-footer-line-1"><span class="post-author vcard">Posted by <span class="fn" itemprop="author" itemscope="itemscope" itemtype="http://schema.org/Person"> <a class="g-profile" data-gapiattached="true" data-gapiscan="true" data-onload="true" href="https://plus.google.com/101726604534965277558" rel="author" title="author profile"><span itemprop="name">Alice Linsley</span></a></span></span><span class="post-timestamp">at  <a class="timestamp-link" href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/2010/09/righteous-job-and-his-kin.html" rel="bookmark" title="permanent link"><abbr class="published" itemprop="datePublished" title="2010-09-26T03:30:00-04:00">3:30 AM</abbr></a></span><span class="reaction-buttons"></span><span class="star-ratings"></span><span class="post-comment-link"></span><span class="post-backlinks post-comment-link"></span><span class="post-icons"><span class="item-action"><a href="http://www.blogger.com/email-post.g?blogID=688820610845171516&amp;postID=1673199973007474148" title="Email Post"><img alt="" class="icon-action" src="/images/imported/img1-blogblog-com/icon18-email-19eb01103c9c.gif" height="13" width="18" /></a></span> </span><br /><div class="post-share-buttons goog-inline-block"></div></div><div class="post-footer-line post-footer-line-2"><span class="post-labels">Labels: <a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/search/label/Horites" rel="tag">Horites</a>, <a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/search/label/Job" rel="tag">Job</a>, <a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/search/label/Naamah" rel="tag">Naamah</a>, <a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/search/label/Teman" rel="tag">Teman</a>, <a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/search/label/Uz" rel="tag">Uz</a></span></div><br /><br /><a href="http://www.biblicalfulfillment.org/id81.html" target="_blank">http://www.biblicalfulfillment.org/id81.html</a><br /><br /><blockquote class="tr_bq"><div class="MsoNormal" style="text-indent: 48px;"><span style="color: #000066; font-family: Arial,Helvetica,sans-serif; font-size: x-small;"><span class="Apple-style-span" style="font-family: Arial; font-size: 16px;">The first chapter states him to have lived in the land of Uz                                     {or Uts}, and in view of ancient history and Biblical evidence, I believe this                                     place to have been located in Arabia of Petraea, on a line between Egypt and                                     Philistia, surrounded by Kedar, Teman and Midan, all of which were districts of                                     Arabia; situated in Idumea, the land of Edom, or Esau; and comprising a large part                                     of it, that Idumea and Ausitis, or the land of Uz, and the land of Edom, were                                     convertible terms, and equally employed to import the same region. Thus Lam.                                     4:21-22, states â€œRejoice and be glad ye daughters of Edom who dwells in the                                     land of Uz, but the cup will come around to you as well, you will become drunk                                     and make your self naked. O daughter of Zion.â€ Who would have thought that the                                     Church of Christ would have been among such wicked nations? The symbolic word                                     â€œZionâ€ has always been recognized as the Church of Christ, or the heavenly                                     Jerusalem. Britannica World Language Dictionary, page 1520.</span></span></div><div class="MsoNormal" style="text-indent: 48px;"><span style="color: #000066; font-family: Arial,Helvetica,sans-serif; font-size: x-small;"><span class="Apple-style-span" style="font-family: Arial; font-size: 16px;">This helps us to better understand the                                     faith of Job, and of                                     his worship to God. Nothing is clearer than that all persons introduced into                                     this work were Idumeans, dwelling in Idumea; or in other words Edomite Arabs.                                     The persons are Job himself dwelling in the land of Uz; Eliphaz of Teman, a                                     district of Arabia, in view of Ezek. 25:13; Amos 1:11, 12, and Obidah 8:9; Jer.                                     49:7-20 Teman was a principle part of Idumea. Eliphaz the son of Esau Gen.                                     36:10 Bildad the Shuah, are always mentioned in connection with Sheba and                                     Dedan, all of them being placed in the vicinity of Idumea. Zophar of Naamah, a                                     city whose name is mentioned in Joshua 15:21-41, and shows it to have been                                     situated in Idumea, and to have lain in a southern direction towards its coast,                                     or the shores of the Red Sea. Elihu of Buz, a name which occurs only once in                                     the sacred Scriptures, but where in Jeremiah 25:22 are mentioned in connection                                     with Teman and Dedan; like themselves a border city called Horitis, Uz, or                                     Idumea, it had a number of names; it was first called Horitis, from the Horites                                     who appear to have first settled there, among these the most distinguished was                                     Seir; and from him the land was known by the name of the land of Seir. The                                     chief had numerious family, and among the most distinguished of his grandsons                                     were Uz, or Uts; and from him, not from the Uz of Nahor the land was called                                     Ausitis, or the land of Uz, the family of Hor, Seir and Uz were at length                                     disposed of the entire region by Esau, or Edom; who strengthened himself by his                                     marriage to the daughter of Ishmael, Gen. 28:9, and the conquered territory was                                     called Idumea, or the land of Edom. I think this to be conclusive as to the                                     country of Job and his friends.</span></span></div><div class="MsoNormal" style="text-indent: 48px;"><span style="color: #000066; font-family: Arial,Helvetica,sans-serif; font-size: x-small;"><span class="Apple-style-span" style="font-family: Arial; font-size: 16px;">It is reasonable in view of historical, and Biblical                                     evidence given above to assume that the two Jobâ€™s mentioned in some history are                                     one and the same person. Since Esau conquered the land of Arabia, and the land                                     was called Edom, and since Eliphaz was the son of Easu, and a cousin to Job, it                                     is most likely the same Eliphaz from Teman, a province of Arabia.</span></span></div><div class="MsoNormal" style="text-indent: 48px;"><span style="color: #000066; font-family: Arial,Helvetica,sans-serif; font-size: x-small;"><span class="Apple-style-span" style="font-family: Arial; font-size: 16px;">Bildad the Shuhite, also                                     inhabited a province of Aribia,                                     this nation of people which inhabited this province began with the children of                                     Abrahamâ€™s last wife Gen. 25:1-6, and later called Edom when Esau the son of                                     Jacob conquered it. Is important at this point to fully understand that the                                     people of this particular area were closely related to Abraham. Ishmael was                                     given this land by God Gen. 21:14-21, and through his seed great nations arose.                                     Esau the grandson of Abraham conquered this land from the children of his                                     grandfather Abraham, and married the daughter, or granddaughter of Ishmael.</span></span></div><div class="MsoNormal" style="text-indent: 48px;"><span style="color: #000066; font-family: Arial,Helvetica,sans-serif; font-size: x-small;"><span class="Apple-style-span" style="font-family: Arial; font-size: 16px;">According to Joshua, Naamah                                     was located between the land of                                     Judah, and that of Edom. As to Zophar nothing more are known, other than that                                     which are recorded in the book of Job.</span></span></div></blockquote><br /><a href="http://draft.blogger.com/goog_910639650"><br /></a><a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/2010/06/horite-confederation-of-uz-buz-and-huz.html" target="_blank">http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/2010/06/horite-confederation-of-uz-buz-and-huz.html</a><br /><br /><h2 class="date-header">Monday, June 14, 2010</h2><a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="2681631280066597258"></a> <br /><h3 class="post-title entry-title" itemprop="name">The Horite Confederation of Uz, Buz and Huz </h3><div class="post-header"></div><div class="post-body entry-content" id="post-body-2681631280066597258" itemprop="description articleBody"><div class="separator" style="clear: both; text-align: left;">Alice C. Linsleyï»¿</div><div class="separator" style="clear: both; text-align: center;"><br /></div><div class="separator" style="clear: both; text-align: center;"><a href="/images/imported/blogger-googleusercontent-com/dedan-map-e42cc9da3375.jpg" style="margin-left: 1em; margin-right: 1em;"><img border="0" src="/images/imported/blogger-googleusercontent-com/dedan-map-26714f50a39c.jpg" height="357" width="400" /></a></div><div class="separator" style="clear: both; text-align: center;">Dedan: <a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/2009/05/horite-territory.html">Horite Territory</a>&nbsp;at the heart of <a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/2009/03/edens-flood-east-and-west.html">Eden</a></div><br /><br /><br />According to Genesis 10:7 and Genesis 25:3, Dedan was descended from Kush and from Abraham by his <a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/2008/06/keturah-wife-or-concubine.html">cousin wife, Keturah</a>.  Dedan's father was Abraham's first-born son Joktan. &nbsp;Dedan's brother  was Sheba the Younger. Diagrammed, Dedan's immediate family looks like  this:<br /><br /><br />&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; Abraham âˆ† = O Keturah <br />&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; Joktan<br />&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; O = âˆ† = O<br />&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; &nbsp;<b>&nbsp; Dedan</b> <b>âˆ†&nbsp;</b>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; âˆ† Sheba the Younger<br /><br /><br />Isaiah 21:13 alludes to the "caravans of Dedanites" in Arabia, and  Ezekiel 27:20 speaks&nbsp;of Dedan&nbsp;as supplying Tyre with precious things.  Dedan is associated with Uz in the hill country of Edom.&nbsp;Uz was the  homeland of Job. One of Job's inquisitors, Elihu, was&nbsp;a descendent of  Nahor by Buz.&nbsp;&nbsp;Buz and Uz were&nbsp;Nahor's sons by Milcah (Gen. 22:20). This  is Uz the Elder whose grandson (by his daughter) was <a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/2010/02/two-named-esau.html">Uz the son of Dishan</a> (I Chron. 1:42). Dishan was a son of Seir the Horite and the  brother-in-law of Esau the Younger. Uz the Younger was Seir's grandson.  Here is Seir's Horite&nbsp;family:<br /><br /><div class="separator" style="clear: both; text-align: center;"><a href="/images/imported/blogger-googleusercontent-com/gen36-of-esau-d0f4ac2d6799.gif" style="margin-left: 1em; margin-right: 1em;"><img border="0" src="/images/imported/blogger-googleusercontent-com/gen36-of-esau-dab2997a8bb3.gif" height="160" width="640" /></a></div><br />When there are two names that are very close, there is usually a third,&nbsp;  That third is Huz, so that Uz, Buz and Huz represent another 3-part  confederation based on kinship.&nbsp; I Chronicles 5:14 mentions that the son  of Buz was Jahdo and Jahdo's son was Yeshishai, the Aramaic&nbsp;form of  Yeshua/Jesus.&nbsp; This connects the name of Jesus with the devotees of  Horus, who was called "Son of God".<br /><br />Buz is related to Uz and is grouped with the peoples of Dedan and Tema  in Jeremiah 25. This is probably why this Horite confederation is not  identified as Uz, Huz and Buz, but rather as Dedan, Tema and Buz.&nbsp;The <a href="http://www.oxfordbiblicalstudies.com/article/opr/t256/e910">oldest Arabic script</a> emanated from the Afro-Arabian oases of Tema and Dedan in <a href="http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hejaz">the Hijaz</a>.  Tema is known by Arabs as Taima and&nbsp;lies about 70 miles north-east of  Dedan. Tema, Dedan and Dumah were caravan stops along&nbsp;the&nbsp;trade route  from Babylon to Sheba.<br /><br />These peoples&nbsp;shared appearance and life style.&nbsp;They dwelt in hills and  built shrines in&nbsp;caves (such as seen at Petra)&nbsp;and the men shaved their  heads (Jeremiah 25:23), as did Horite priests.&nbsp;This suggests that this  was a confederation of Horite priestly families.&nbsp; Genesis 36 confirms  this, listing Uz's grandson Dedan as a Horite ruler.&nbsp; Here we also find  reference to Huz or Husham of the land of Tema (Gen. 36:34).<br /><br />Other 3-part familial confederations are suggested by the names Sab-tah  (Gen. 10:6) and Sab-teca (Gen. 10:7), and Le-hab (Gen. 10:13), Le-sha  (Gen. 10:19) and Le-tu (Gen. 25:3). Letu was an&nbsp;eighth generation  descendent of Noah through his sons <a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/2009/03/sheba-lines-of-ham-and-shem.html">Shem and Ham whose lines intermarried</a>.<br /><br />The prominence of the Horites is attested by the detail of the  geneological information provided in Genesis 36 and I Chronicles 1. Here  we discover that a people that have been regarded as tangentially  related to Abraham were&nbsp;in fact Abraham's people. Isaac married Horite  brides. Rebekah (his cousin bride) was a descedent of Na'Hor and Terah,  and his half-sister bride (living in Beersheba) was a descendant of  Sheba. Jacob also married Horite brides in Rachel and Leah, both  descendants of Na'Hor and Terah. Genesis 36 tells us that <a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/2010/02/two-named-esau.html">Esau the Younger</a> married a Oholibamah.<br /><br /><br />Related reading:&nbsp; <a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/2010/10/abraham-and-job-horite-rulers.html">Abraham and Job: Horite Rulers</a>; <a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/2010/09/abrahams-nephews-and-niece.html">Abraham's Nephews and Niece</a>; <a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/2011/01/who-were-horites.html">Who Were the Horites?</a> </div><div class="post-footer-line post-footer-line-1"><span class="post-author vcard">Posted by <span class="fn" itemprop="author" itemscope="itemscope" itemtype="http://schema.org/Person"> <a class="g-profile" data-gapiattached="true" data-gapiscan="true" data-onload="true" href="https://plus.google.com/101726604534965277558" rel="author" title="author profile"><span itemprop="name">Alice Linsley</span></a></span></span><span class="post-timestamp">at  <a class="timestamp-link" href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/2010/06/horite-confederation-of-uz-buz-and-huz.html" rel="bookmark" title="permanent link"><abbr class="published" itemprop="datePublished" title="2010-06-14T09:15:00-04:00">9:15 AM</abbr></a></span><span class="reaction-buttons"></span><span class="star-ratings"></span><span class="post-comment-link"></span><span class="post-backlinks post-comment-link"></span><span class="post-icons"><span class="item-action"><a href="http://www.blogger.com/email-post.g?blogID=688820610845171516&amp;postID=2681631280066597258" title="Email Post"><img alt="" class="icon-action" src="/images/imported/img1-blogblog-com/icon18-email-19eb01103c9c.gif" height="13" width="18" /></a></span> </span><br /><div class="post-share-buttons goog-inline-block"></div></div><div class="post-footer-line post-footer-line-2"><span class="post-labels">Labels: <a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/search/label/Buz" rel="tag">Buz</a>, <a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/search/label/Horites" rel="tag">Horites</a>, <a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/search/label/Huz" rel="tag">Huz</a>, <a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/search/label/three%20sons" rel="tag">three sons</a>, <a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/search/label/Uz" rel="tag">Uz</a></span></div><a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/2010/10/abraham-and-job-horite-rulers.html" target="_blank"><br /></a><a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/2010/10/abraham-and-job-horite-rulers.html" target="_blank">http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/2010/10/abraham-and-job-horite-rulers.html </a><br /><h2 class="date-header">Monday, October 4, 2010</h2><a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="7096674744305485062"></a> <br /><h3 class="post-title entry-title" itemprop="name">Abraham and Job: Horite Rulers </h3><div class="post-header"></div><br />Alice C. Linsley<br /><br /><br /><div class="MsoNormal" style="margin: 0in 0in 10pt;"><span style="font-family: 'Times New Roman', serif; font-size: 12pt;">Job was an historical person, as&nbsp;</span><a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/2010/09/righteous-job-and-his-kin.html">the genealogical information in the Bible makes clear</a>. He was the great grandson of Nahor, Abraham's brother. His maternal grandfather was Nahor's son, Uz the Elder.<br /><br />According to I Chronicles 1:42, the elder Uz's daughter married Dishan,  the Horite. She named their first-born son Uz after her father,  according to the <a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/2007/04/brides-naming-prerogative.html">cousin bride's naming prerogative</a>.<br /><br />Uz the Younger was Seir's grandson. Here is Seir's Horite family:</div><br /><div class="separator" style="clear: both; text-align: center;"><a href="/images/imported/blogger-googleusercontent-com/seir-the-horite-430f23a54c88.gif" style="margin-left: 1em; margin-right: 1em;"><img border="0" src="/images/imported/blogger-googleusercontent-com/seir-the-horite-c67d6a309c7d.gif" height="160px" width="640px" /></a></div><br /><span style="font-family: 'Times New Roman', serif; font-size: 12pt;"><br /></span><span style="font-family: 'Times New Roman', serif; font-size: 12pt;">Job was of the clan of Uz. He was <a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/2012/07/origins-of-word-horite.html">a Horite</a>.  The Horites were a caste of rulers who controlled the trade routes.  Originally, they controlled the major waters systems at a time when the  Sahara, Arabia and Mesopotamia were wetter. They were <a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/2010/01/blood-and-crosses.html">devotees of Horus</a>, who was born miraculously of Hathor-Meri by the overshadowing of the Sun (the Creator's emblem).&nbsp;</span><br /><span style="font-family: 'Times New Roman', serif; font-size: 12pt;"><br /></span><span style="font-family: 'Times New Roman', serif; font-size: 12pt;">The  Horites served as shrine and temple attendants and many were priests  who interceded for others and offered sacrifice. Job offered sacrifice  daily for the sins of his own family. At the end of the book, God tells  Job to pray for his kinsmen Eliphaz, Zophar and Bildad.&nbsp;This is  reminiscent of Abraham praying for&nbsp;Abimelech and his whole household  (Gen. 20:17,18).</span><br /><br /><span style="font-family: 'Times New Roman', serif; font-size: 12pt;">Job's  people were associated with the Dedanites and the Temanites. Clearly  the author of the book of Job was writing well&nbsp;after Job's time&nbsp;because  he ridicules Job and his kin <span style="font-family: 'Times New Roman', serif; font-size: 12pt;">as  people from the desolate wilderness who live in the clefts of the  valleys and in the caves.&nbsp;&nbsp;He portrays them&nbsp;as "donkeys braying among  the bushes" (Job 30:3-7). This is not how Job himself would have  regarded his kin.&nbsp; We can be fairly certain that the author of Job was  not someone who lived in that part of Canaan that was</span></span> <a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/2009/05/horite-territory.html">Horite territory</a>.<br /><span style="font-family: 'Times New Roman', serif; font-size: 12pt;"><span style="font-family: 'Times New Roman', serif; font-size: 12pt;"><br /></span></span><span style="font-family: 'Times New Roman', serif; font-size: 12pt;"><span style="font-family: 'Times New Roman', serif; font-size: 12pt;">There is a very old tradition that the God of the Hebrews (<i><a href="http://biblicalanthropology.blogspot.com/2010/09/who-were-hapiru.html">haBiru</a></i>) "came from Teman. This is reflected in Habakkuk 3:3 which states, "</span></span>God came from Teman, the Holy One from Mount Paran." This is one reason that Frank Moore <a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/2011/10/frank-moore-cross-israels-god-is-god-of.html">Cross believes that the God of Israel is the God of the Horites</a>.<br /><br /><div class="MsoNormal" style="margin: 0in 0in 10pt;"><span style="font-family: 'Times New Roman', serif; font-size: 12pt;">Job and his&nbsp;kin dwelt in the&nbsp;hill country of Canaan and built shrines in caves such as at Petra. It is not surprising that <a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/2012/07/petra-reflects-horite-beliefs.html">Petra reflects Horite beliefs</a>.&nbsp;</span><br /><span style="font-family: 'Times New Roman', serif; font-size: 12pt;"><br /></span><span style="font-family: 'Times New Roman', serif; font-size: 12pt;">Horite ruler-priests shaved their heads (Jeremiah 25:23), as was the custom&nbsp;of Horite priests. Esau the Younger had a son named <i>Korah</i> which means shaved one.[1] This suggests that this was a confederation  of Horite priestly families. Genesis 36 confirms this, listing Uz's  grandson Dedan as a Horite ruler. Here we also find reference to Huz or  Husham of the land of Tema (Gen. 36:34).</span><br /><br /><div class="MsoNormal" style="margin: 0in 0in 10pt;"><span style="font-family: 'Times New Roman', serif; font-size: 12pt;">The people of Uz belonged to a 3-clan Horite confederation.[2] Their kin and allies were the Dedanites and the Temanites. </span><span style="font-family: 'Times New Roman', serif; font-size: 12pt;">The  clan of Teman descended from the elder Esau's son Teman&nbsp;(Gen. 36:11).&nbsp;  The Temanites were known for their wisdom. Job's friend Eliphaz was a  Temanite. Jeremiah 49:7 speaks of the wisdom of Teman and verse 8&nbsp;links  the Temanites with the Dedanites. The largest collection of texts in the  oldest Arabic script come from Tema and Dedan in the Hijaz. Tema is  known by Arabs as Taima and lies about 70 miles north-east of Dedan.  Tema, Dedan and Dumah were caravan stops along the trade route from  Sheba to Babylon.</span></div><span style="font-family: 'Times New Roman', serif; font-size: 12pt;"><span style="font-family: 'Times New Roman', serif; font-size: 12pt;">According  to Genesis 10:7, Dedan the Elder was descended from Kush, the son of  Ham. According to Genesis 25:3,&nbsp;Dedan the Younger&nbsp;was a descendant of  Abraham&nbsp;and his cousin wife, Keturah. Dedan's father was <a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/2008/10/abrahams-first-born-son.html"><span style="color: #b45f06;">Abraham's first-born son Joktan</span></a>.  Isaiah 21:13 speaks&nbsp;of the "caravans of Dedanites" in Arabia, and  Ezekiel 27:20 speaks of Dedan as supplying Tyre with precious things.  Dedan is associated with Uz in the hill country of Edom, Job's  homeland.&nbsp; This is Uz the Elder, son of Nahor, whose grandson (by his  daughter) was Uz, the son of Dishan, shown in diagram above.</span></span></div><br /><span style="font-family: 'Times New Roman', serif; font-size: 12pt;">NOTES</span><br /><br />1. Moses had a half-brother named Korah. (See Moses' family diagram  below.) Shaving the body was the custom for Horite priests in Egypt. See  Biblical Archaeology Review, July/August 2007, p.37. According to  Numbers 26, Korah's claim to be the ruler was supported by the  Hanochites (descendents of <a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/2007/11/where-did-cain-find-his-wife.html"><span style="color: #de7008;">Nok</span></a> through Jacob's first-born son Reuben). The diagram below shows the  typical marriage pattern of Horite rulers. Amram had two wives. One was a  half-sister (Jochebed) and the other was either a patrilineal cousin or  niece (Ishar).<br /><br /><div class="separator" style="clear: both; text-align: center;"><a href="https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgfryHgwUNN9v3k5NGXHUbkAwLJ89fZj7e2tJZUgvK74_uDfLbksC9WbNLjHd9slIGQ3hHw0-7K0Yn77-1YVh-s0CxVgEVdbuKv622OODafJ6_PECrzLJWbmO2CbLlO0KOtxbxaRjyQ52lH/s1600/Moses'+Family.jpg" style="margin-left: 1em; margin-right: 1em;"><img border="0" src="https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEgfryHgwUNN9v3k5NGXHUbkAwLJ89fZj7e2tJZUgvK74_uDfLbksC9WbNLjHd9slIGQ3hHw0-7K0Yn77-1YVh-s0CxVgEVdbuKv622OODafJ6_PECrzLJWbmO2CbLlO0KOtxbxaRjyQ52lH/s320/Moses'+Family.jpg" height="294px" width="320px" /></a></div><br /><span style="font-family: 'Times New Roman', serif; font-size: 12pt;">2. The Ho</span><span style="font-family: 'Times New Roman', serif; font-size: 12pt;">rite confederation is not identified as Uz, Huz and Buz, but rather as Dedan, Tema and Buz. </span><br /><span style="font-family: 'Times New Roman', serif; font-size: 12pt;"><br /></span><span style="font-family: 'Times New Roman', serif; font-size: 12pt;"><br /></span><span style="font-family: 'Times New Roman', serif; font-size: 12pt;">Related reading: <a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/2013/04/the-calling-of-abraham.html">The Calling of Abraham</a>;&nbsp;<a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/2012/08/the-horite-ancestry-of-jesus-christ_8.html">The Horite Ancestry of Jesus Christ</a>;&nbsp;<a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/2012/07/petra-reflects-horite-beliefs.html">Petra Reflects Horite Beliefs</a>; <a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/2012/07/origins-of-word-horite.html">Etymology of the Word Horite</a>; <a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/2011/01/who-were-horites.html">Who Were the Horites?</a>; <a href="http://jandyongenesis.blogspot.com/2010/09/dedanites.html">The Afro-Arabian Dedanites</a>; <a href="http://www.communitychurchva.info/Forms/BookofJOB.pdf">Job's Friends and Their Contribution to the Message of Job</a></span><br /><!-- Blogger automated replacement: "https://images-blogger-opensocial.googleusercontent.com/gadgets/proxy?url=http%3A%2F%2F3.bp.blogspot.com%2F_gydC7NpnG8I%2FTJ58S8KtFFI%2FAAAAAAAABmA%2FVPUuoIt8NNM%2Fs640%2Fgen36%2Bof%2BEsau.gif&amp;container=blogger&amp;gadget=a&amp;rewriteMime=image%2F*" with "/images/imported/blogger-googleusercontent-com/gen36-of-esau-88e82c26d229.gif" -->
